title: The Hollow Choir
author: Mon D. Ong
description: At the Ashencroft Academy, children with the Spark train to become mages. But those who fail the Trials are never seen again. Elara soon discovers that the school’s choir sings with voices that are not their own — and she is the next verse.

content.text: The carriage had no driver. Elara discovered this on the first morning, when she had mustered the courage to push aside the moth-eaten velvet curtain and peer out. The driver's seat was empty, the reins held by no human hand, yet the two black horses trotted on with a steady, mechanical rhythm. Their hooves made no sound on the packed earth, and when the lantern that swung from the carriage's corner caught their eyes, she saw they were the flat, milk-white of the blind. The sight rooted her to the worn leather seat, her heart a cold stone in her chest.

For three days she had been alone in that creaking box, the only passenger. Outside, the world had shed its colour like a snake discarding an old skin. The rolling green hills of her homeland, dotted with sheep and the occasional village chimney, had first given way to grey rock and stunted thorn bushes. By the second day, the soil itself had turned black, crumbling like ancient charcoal, and the sky had become a perpetual, bruised twilight. No birds sang. No insects chittered. The only sound was the whisper of the wind, which seemed to carry fragments of voices that vanished as soon as she tried to focus on them.

She clutched the letter. The parchment was the colour of dried flower petals, smooth and cool to the touch, and the ink was a deep, arterial red. It had arrived on the morning of her eleventh birthday, not delivered by any postman but simply placed on her pillow while she slept. The village elder, a man named Cassian who had known her grandmother, had come to her small cottage as she was reading it. He was a tall, stooped man with a face like cracked leather, and when he saw the letter in her hands, he sat down heavily on the doorstep and wept. Not a few polite tears, but a raw, gasping grief that shook his shoulders. 'Come back,' he had whispered, when he could speak. 'If you can. Promise me you will try to come back.' Elara had not understood then. She did now.

content.caption: The black shore where the carriage stopped, and the distant spire.

content.text: The carriage stopped abruptly, throwing her forward. When she scrambled to the window, she saw that they had reached the edge of a cliff. Beyond it, a churning grey sea gnawed at jagged rocks, and in the distance, wreathed in coils of mist, rose a single tower from an island of black stone. It was not a friendly tower. It looked like a tooth torn from some buried giant, sharp and spiteful against the blank sky. A narrow stone pier jutted into the water, and at its end waited a ferry. The ferrymen were hooded, their faces invisible, and they stood motionless as the carriage door opened on its own. Elara stepped out, her small bag of belongings clutched to her chest, and the cold hit her — not just the damp chill of the sea, but a deeper cold, the kind that settles in the marrow.

No one spoke. The ferrymen did not acknowledge her except to extend a gloved hand for the letter. She gave it, and they nodded once, their cowls dipping. The crossing was silent. The water slapped against the hull with a sound like wet leather, and the mist curled around her, carrying that ever-present hum, a note just below hearing that seemed to vibrate in her teeth. As they approached the island, the tower resolved into a sprawling fortress of black stone, with walls that climbed like a frozen scream. Lanterns burned at intervals along the ramparts, but their flames were a sickly, pale green, casting shadows that seemed to move independently of the light.

When the ferry scraped against the dock, she saw the gates. They were immense, wrought iron, and set between two pillars carved in the likeness of weeping women. Their stone tears had worn channels down their cheeks, and Elara could not shake the feeling that they were crying real water. She passed through the gates and into a courtyard, and there she stopped dead. Lining the path were statues of children — life-sized, posed as if caught mid-step or mid-cry. Their faces were frozen in expressions of shock, their hands raised as if to ward off something unseen. The detail was uncanny: freckles, cowlicks, the stitching on a shoe. 'They are not statues,' a voice said beside her. Elara jumped. A boy about her age, with dark circles under his eyes and a battered violin case strapped to his back, stood regarding her with a grim sort of sympathy. 'They're what happens when you try to run. Or so they say. I'm Ren.' He didn't offer a hand, just turned and walked toward the inner doors. Elara, after a moment, followed.

The Great Hall swallowed them. It was vast, cavernous, lit by thousands of those greenish lanterns. Dozens of children stood in loose clusters, all looking as lost and frightened as she felt. Some were crying softly; others stood rigid with bravado they couldn't quite maintain. At the head of the hall, on a throne that seemed to be carved from fused bone, sat the Headmaster. Archmage Kael was beautiful in the way a glacier is beautiful: ancient, remote, and utterly indifferent. His hair was white, not with age but with the colour of deep frost, and his eyes were a pale, piercing blue that seemed to look through her rather than at her. When he spoke, his voice did not come from his lips. It emerged from the walls, the floor, the very air — a layered, resonant sound that vibrated in her breastbone. 'Welcome, little flames. You are Embers now, children who carry the Spark. You will be trained. You will be tested. You will burn, or you will be extinguished. The Academy does not suffer ash.' Elara's hands trembled. Beside her, Ren's jaw was tight, his knuckles white on his violin strap. That night, after they had been shown to cold stone dormitories and fed a thin, grey stew that tasted of nothing, Elara heard the singing for the first time. It drifted through the stone like water through cloth, a choir of children's voices, wordless, weaving a melody that was both lullaby and lament. It made her chest ache, and she fell asleep with tears on her cheeks, not knowing why.

Breakfast was a grim affair. The long tables in the dining hall were set with bowls of the same grey stew and hard, black bread that tasted faintly of ash. The older students, those who had survived previous Trials, ate in silence, their eyes downcast. They wore robes of varying colours — deep blue, pale green, sometimes a rich crimson — and Elara noticed that they moved around each other with a careful, practised distance, as if proximity itself were dangerous. Ren sat across from her, methodically crumbling his bread without eating it. 'They sort us today,' he said, his voice flat. 'The Resonance Test. It decides everything.'

She learned more from a girl named Orin, who had a pinched, hungry face and a nervous habit of twisting her fingers. 'My brother was here two years ago,' Orin whispered, leaning close. 'He wrote home at first. Said the Resonance Test placed him in the lower tiers. Then the letters stopped. When I got my own summons, my parents... they wouldn't look at me. They know what happens to the low-tone Embers.' Elara wanted to ask more, but a bell tolled — a single, deep note that seemed to come from everywhere and made her bones hum — and the entire hall rose as one. It was time.

They were led into the depths of the academy, down spiralling staircases that seemed to go on forever. The air grew colder, damper, and the walls began to glisten with moisture. Finally, they emerged into a vast, circular chamber of polished obsidian. The ceiling soared into darkness, and at its centre hung a tuning fork the size of a grown man, suspended by silver chains. Its surface was etched with runes that writhed when she tried to look at them directly. A panel of examiners sat behind a stone table — five figures in grey robes, their faces hidden by featureless masks. The masks had no eye holes, yet Elara felt their gaze on her like a weight.

One by one, the Embers were called forward. An examiner would strike the great tuning fork with a mallet of black crystal, and the resulting tone would fill the chamber — a deep, thrumming wave that made the air shimmer. The child would then be instructed to sing a single note, any note, and the interaction between their voice and the fork's resonance would reveal their Spark's nature. Elara watched, her heart pounding. A boy named Tomas, with bright, eager eyes, produced a clear, bell-like tone that hung in the air like a star. The examiners nodded, and he was ushered to a group on the left, where children in colourful robes waited. A girl named Lissa, who had been trembling, managed only a thin, wavering note that was swallowed by the fork's hum. There was a long, terrible pause, and then an examiner spoke a single word: 'Low-tier.' Lissa's face crumpled, and she was taken by two hooded attendants through a door that Elara had not noticed before — a small, iron door set into the far wall. She did not come back.

When Elara's name was called, her legs felt like they belonged to someone else. She walked to the centre of the chamber, the tuning fork looming above her. The mallet struck, and the tone hit her like a physical wave, vibrating in her chest, in her skull. She opened her mouth and sang. She had never thought of herself as a singer, but the note that emerged was... strange. It was not one note. It was a chord, a layering of multiple tones that seemed to braid together in the air. The examiners straightened. The one who had spoken before leaned forward, and even through the mask, Elara sensed a sharp, calculating interest. 'Indeterminate,' the examiner said. Another added, 'Chromatic aberration.' They murmured among themselves, and the word 'vessel' floated to her ears, though she did not then know what it meant. She was given grey robes — neither the coloured robes of the high tiers nor the white of the low — and placed in a drafty cell of a room in the Spire's middle tier. Ren, who had produced a tone that was rich and warm but with a slight edge of dissonance, was placed in the room next to hers. 'Grey,' he said later, with a bitter smile. 'It means they don't know what to do with us. It's the safest colour to wear. For now.'

chapters.title: Chapter 3 — The First Trial and the New Voice

content.text: The days that followed were a blur of lessons: Harmony Theory, Resonant Incantations, Aural History. The teachers were hollow-eyed and spoke in monotones, and the textbooks were filled with passages that had been deliberately blacked out. The choir sang every night, and every night Elara fell asleep with that aching, wordless grief pressing against her ribs. She began to notice that the voices in the choir were not always the same. New ones appeared, raw and trembling, while others faded or changed. One morning, she woke to find that the bed in the room across the hall, which had belonged to a quiet girl named Sari, was stripped bare. No one mentioned Sari's name again. But that night, a new soprano trembled through the stone — high, frightened, the voice of a girl who had been very, very afraid. It was Sari's voice. Elara knew it.

The First Trial came without warning. A bell tolled at midnight, and the Embers were herded from their beds, still in their nightclothes, down into a vast arena beneath the academy. The arena floor was a labyrinth of shifting stone walls, and at its centre burned a brazier of cold, blue-white fire that gave off no heat. 'Reach the flame,' a disembodied voice instructed. 'The shadows are hungry. Feed them with your Spark, or they will feed on you.' Then the gates slammed shut, and the children were alone in the maze.

Elara ran. The walls moved with a grinding, tectonic slowness, rearranging the path ahead and behind. In the darkness, things stirred — amalgamations of smoke and jagged teeth that coalesced from the shadows themselves. They recoiled from any flare of magic but lunged at hesitation. She saw a boy named Kip, who had been boasting of his Spark earlier, try to hurl a bolt of light at one of the creatures. The light sputtered, the shadow swallowed it, and Kip's scream was brief, muffled, and then gone. The choir gained a new tenor that night. Elara ran harder, her lungs burning, and when she finally stumbled into the chamber of the cold flame, she was one of only twelve. The flame, when she touched it, did not burn. It whispered a name — her grandmother's name, a name she had not heard spoken since she was a toddler. 'Elena,' it breathed. 'She promised. She promised one would return.' Elara pulled her hand back, trembling.

Ren was waiting for her in the corridor after the Trial, his violin case clutched to his chest. He had survived, but barely; a long scratch ran down his cheek, and his eyes were haunted. 'My brother,' he said, without preamble. 'His name was Lian. He was here two years ago. He failed the Third Trial. And I hear him. Every night, in the singing. I know it's him.' His voice cracked, and Elara, still shaking from the cold flame's whisper, made a decision. She would find out the truth. She would go to the Library.

The Library was a spiralling structure that burrowed deep into the island's rock, shelves carved from the living stone. The upper levels held the sanctioned texts — dry tomes on harmonic theory and the ethics of Spark-craft. But a cordoned-off staircase, blocked by chains that hummed with warding magic, led down into the restricted archives. Elara, moving at a time when the halls were empty, discovered that the chains could be silenced if she sang a quiet, layered chord — the same dissonant harmony her voice naturally produced. The chains parted like water, and she descended.

content.caption: The forbidden depths of the library, where old journals were kept.

content.text: Down there, in the cold and the dark, she found a journal bound in something that was certainly not leather — it was too warm, too smooth, and it pulsed faintly under her fingers. It belonged to a boy named Theron, who had been a student seventy years ago. His entries spoke of the 'Transfers' — children who failed the Trials and were 'given to the Choir'. But Theron had discovered the truth. The children were not simply killed. Their souls were extracted, their voices peeled from their throats while they still lived, and woven into a massive spell that sustained the Headmaster's youth. 'He has lived for centuries,' Theron wrote, the handwriting becoming more frantic with each page. 'He drinks our sparks like wine. The Choir is his cellar, and we are the vintage.' The final entry was barely legible, the ink smeared by what looked like water drops but which Elara knew, with a sick certainty, were tears. 'I am to be Transferred tomorrow. If anyone reads this, know that Kael is not a man. He is a parasite wearing a beautiful shell. The only way to stop him is to shatter the song from within. I have found a counter-spell, but it requires a voice that can hold multiple tones — a layered chord. I do not have such a voice. Perhaps one day, someone will.' Tucked into the back cover was a thin sheet of vellum covered in musical notation. Elara's hands shook as she read it. The counter-spell was written for her. For someone exactly like her.

Elara brought the journal to Ren and to a teacher she had cautiously begun to trust: Mistress Solaine, a Harmony instructor whose own daughter, Isla, had been taken by the Choir two years ago. Solaine was a gaunt woman with eyes like burnt-out candles, but when Elara showed her the counter-spell, something flickered in those eyes — a spark of fierce, desperate hope. 'This could work,' she whispered, tracing the notation with a trembling finger. 'But you would have to go into the Chamber of Voices. The heart of the spell. And you would need to sing this... while they are all trying to stop you.'

They planned the descent during the Convergence Eve, the one night each year when Kael retreated to his sanctum to perform a ritual that renewed the wards around the island. The entire academy would be at the ceremony in the Great Hall above, leaving the catacombs relatively unguarded. Ren would play a harmonic counterpoint on his violin, a frequency that Mistress Solaine calculated could temporarily disrupt the binding of the crystal heart. Elara would sing the dissonant counter-spell, and Solaine would use her own voice to shield them from the choir's retaliatory resonance. It was a desperate plan, with a hundred ways to fail.

The catacombs were a labyrinth of bone-white roots and phosphorescent moss that glowed with a faint, sickly luminescence. The air grew thick with the sound of the choir — no longer distant, but a tangible pressure, a thousand small hands pressing against their skin. They moved through tunnels that branched and twisted, and more than once they had to hide as shambling, grey-robed attendants — the same ones who had taken the low-tier children — passed by, their faces still hidden, their movements unnervingly silent. Finally, the tunnel opened into a vast, domed hall. And there, Elara saw the true horror of Ashencroft.

content.caption: The Chamber of Voices, lined with crystal coffins.

content.text: The Chamber of Voices was a cathedral of the dead. Crystal coffins, stacked ten high, lined every wall, and inside each one floated a child. Their eyes were open and vacant, their mouths fixed in an eternal 'O'. From each throat, a thin silver thread rose and converged in the centre of the dome, where a massive crystalline heart beat with a slow, sickly rhythm. The threads were the voices, siphoned and woven into a harmonic prison that pulsed upward, into the academy, into Kael. Mistress Solaine let out a choked sob and rushed to a coffin where a girl with long dark hair floated, a tiny smear of dried blood at the corner of her lips. 'Isla,' she wept, pressing her palms against the crystal. Ren found his brother's coffin. The resemblance was unmistakable — the same curve of the jaw, the same unruly hair. He didn't speak. He simply fell to his knees, his violin slipping from his hands.

The air in the chamber thickened, grew cold. The choir's song faltered, then resumed with a new, discordant edge. Kael materialised from the shadows, his form seeming to absorb the light around him. He was not angry; he was deeply, serenely pleased. 'I had hoped you would come,' he said, his voice still emanating from everywhere, a multi-layered whisper that echoed with the timbres of a hundred dead children. 'You were built for curiosity, Elara. Built to seek the truth. And to walk willingly into the heart of the song.'

He explained everything, with the patient condescension of an immortal addressing a mayfly. The Ashencroft Academy was not a school. It was a soul-farm. The Trials were a sieve, separating the weak from the strong. The weak were harvested immediately. The strong were cultivated, their Sparks allowed to mature, and then they too were taken. But Elara... Elara was different. She was not a normal child. She had not been born in the ordinary way. Kael had, decades ago, extracted the purest resonant notes from the sacrificed children and woven them into a single, perfect vessel. That vessel had been placed in a woman named Elena — her grandmother — who had made a pact with the forest decades before. The child born of that vessel was Elara's mother, and then Elara. She was a living choir, a container designed to hold not one soul, but many. And when Kael's current body finally gave out, he would pour his own ancient consciousness into her, overwriting her entirely. She was his masterpiece, his next century of life.

'You are a chorus unto yourself,' Kael said, his frozen blue eyes gleaming. 'And soon, you will sing only my name.' Ren lunged at him with a shard of crystal torn from a coffin, but Kael waved a hand, and Ren was pinned to the wall by an invisible pressure, his violin clattering to the floor. Mistress Solaine tried a counterspell, her voice rising in a desperate aria, but the choir simply absorbed her notes, twisting them into a mocking harmony. Kael smiled. 'All sound serves me here. You cannot unmake a symphony with a single scream.' Then he turned back to Elara. 'The Convergence is in two days. On that night, I will speak the final incantation, and your soul will be overwritten. I suggest you spend your remaining time making peace with your borrowed existence.' He vanished, leaving them in the humming dark.

They retreated to Mistress Solaine's forgotten storeroom, hidden behind a false wall in the Harmony wing. Ren was shaking, not from fear but from a rage so deep it had nowhere to go. 'We smash the heart,' he said, his voice raw. 'We destroy it tonight.' But Elara, sitting cross-legged on the cold stone, was reading Theron's journal again, her brow furrowed. 'If we shatter the heart now,' she said slowly, 'the souls will be released, but they'll be scattered. Untethered. They'll fade into nothing, lost between worlds. We need a vessel — a focus — to hold them until the Convergence, when the boundaries between realms are thin enough to let them pass beyond.' She looked up, and her eyes, which had always been a plain brown, now held faint flecks of gold. 'I am a vessel. I was made to hold a soul — one soul. But I was also made from many. The layered chord I carry... it's not a flaw. It's a door.'

Mistress Solaine understood, and the horror on her face was immediate. 'If you open yourself to them, you'll be flooded. The sheer multiplicity... it could tear your identity apart. You might not come back.' 'I know,' Elara said. 'But I was never meant to exist anyway. I was meant to be Kael. Perhaps I can be something else instead. Something we choose.' Ren shook his head, his voice cracking. 'No. There has to be another way.' But even as he said it, he knew there wasn't. He picked up his violin, his brother's violin, and began to tune it to the dissonant frequency Solaine had calculated. 'Then you'd better hold on,' he said, not looking at her. 'I didn't carry this thing through hell just to play lullabies.'

content.caption: The stillness before the Convergence, as Elara prepared.

content.text: They spent the next day in frantic preparation. Solaine drilled Elara on the counter-spell, a sequence of layered tones that felt like tearing her own voice in half. Ren practised the harmonic disruption, his fingers bleeding on the strings. And Elara, in the quiet moments, found herself talking to the gold flecks in her eyes — to the fragments of the children who had gone into her making. She didn't hear answers, exactly. But she felt a warmth, a presence, a chorus of barely-there whispers that seemed to say: *We are with you. We have been waiting.*

The Convergence ceremony transformed the Great Hall into a cathedral of black flame and writhing shadow. Every Ember was forced to attend, lined up in rows like offerings. At the altar, before a massive obsidian mirror whose surface rippled like liquid night, stood Kael. He wore robes of white and silver that seemed to drink the light, and his beauty was terrible — a cold, perfect thing that had been preserved for centuries on the voices of children. The choir sang, louder than ever, a triumphant, aching anthem that shook the stones. Elara, dressed in the white robes of the Vessel Ascendant, walked the long aisle toward him. Her heart pounded, but her mind was a chorus of other voices, steadying her.

Kael smiled as she approached. 'You have made peace, I see. Good. It is easier when the vessel is willing.' He raised his hands, and the choir's song swelled. He began the incantation — ancient words that felt like hooks in the air, pulling at the edges of Elara's soul. She felt a terrible tugging, as if something were trying to unspool her from the inside. But deep beneath the Great Hall, in the Chamber of Voices, Ren was playing. His violin's thin, human thread of sound wound up through the stone, a dissonant frequency that struck the crystalline heart. The heart shuddered. The silver threads connecting the coffins faltered.

Kael's smile vanished. 'What—' Then Elara opened her mouth and sang. It was not one note, but a chord, a cascading torrent of sound that held within it the voices of every child in those crystal coffins. The silver threads snapped free and spiralled upward, through the rock, into the Great Hall, and into her. She felt them enter — a flood of memory, of terror, of hope, of names. Mira, Sari, Isla, Theron, Lian, Kip, and a hundred more. They poured into the empty spaces Kael had crafted to hold his own spirit, and they filled her to bursting. Her eyes blazed gold, and when she spoke, her voice was a legion. 'We are not your vessel.'

content.caption: Elara, transformed by the gathered souls, became a living chord of light and sorrow.

content.text: Kael screamed — a sound of pure, ancient terror — and launched himself at her, his form unravelling into a storm of shadow and teeth. But the Chorus within Elara rose as one. A thousand voices spoke a single word, the word of unmaking hidden in Theron's final notation. The word hit Kael like a physical force. His borrowed years fell away in layers; his body crumbled to dust; his soul, ancient and brittle, fragmented into a discordant screech that faded into silence. The obsidian mirror shattered. The green lanterns flickered and died. The Academy shook to its foundations, and the choir — the real choir, the hollow chorus of stolen voices — fell silent for the first time in centuries.

In the catacombs, the crystal coffins cracked open. The bodies inside dissolved into motes of golden light that streamed upward through the stone, through the earth, into the night sky. Ren, his violin still humming, watched his brother's face fade into warmth and luminance. 'Goodbye, Lian,' he whispered, and the light touched his cheek like a hand before it vanished. Mistress Solaine held her daughter's fading form for as long as she could, and then she was holding only air, and light, and a grief that was finally clean enough to cry.

Above, in the shattered Great Hall, Elara collapsed. The gold in her eyes dimmed to flecks, but did not vanish entirely. Ren, scrambling up through the wreckage, found her lying in a circle of broken glass, breathing but still. 'Elara?' He knelt beside her, his voice raw. Her eyes opened. They were still her eyes, still brown, but behind them moved something deep and quiet, a chorus of remembered light. 'We are here,' she said, and the plural was not a mistake. 'They're gone now, mostly. But they left echoes. I remember their names. I remember all of them.' She looked at Ren and smiled, a small, fragile thing. 'Lian says thank you. For the song. He always loved your playing.' Ren laughed, a broken, watery sound, and didn't bother to wipe his tears.

The island was crumbling, the ancient spells that had held it together unravelling without Kael's will. The surviving Embers, the prisoners, the grey-robed attendants now blinking in confusion as their compulsions lifted, all made their way to the ferries. The hooded ferrymen had pulled back their cowls, revealing the weary, grateful faces of former victims who had been bound to service. They helped the children aboard, and the boats pushed off from the jagged rock. Elara stood at the stern, watching the spire recede into the mist. The weeping statues at the gate had stopped weeping. The iron doors had rusted to dust. The Hollow Choir was hollow no more.

They made landfall as dawn broke over a world that felt new. The black shore was now pale sand; the sea was a clear, living blue. The Embers, just children again, huddled on the beach, unsure of what to do with a freedom they had never expected to possess. Mistress Solaine, her face streaked with tears but her spine straight, stood beside Elara. 'We'll need to teach them. A new kind of magic. No more Trials. No more... harvesting.' Elara nodded. 'We'll build a school. A real one. Where Sparks are tended, not consumed.' Ren, his battered violin cradled in his arms, plucked a hopeful note. 'Will there be music?' he asked. Elara turned to him, the gold in her eyes catching the sunrise. 'There will be music. There will always be music.' And somewhere, on the edge of hearing, the Chorus hummed its agreement — a lullaby now, not a lament.

content.caption: The dawn that met them on the other side.
